faqs.question: How do I contact Xinleineng technical support through LiTong Electronics?
faqs.answer: You can contact our technical support team through multiple channels: 1) Submit a support ticket via our website's technical support portal, 2) Email our FAE team directly at fae@litong.com, 3) Call our technical hotline during business hours, or 4) Contact your assigned sales representative who can escalate technical questions. For urgent production issues, please mark your inquiry as 'Urgent' for priority response within 4 hours.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for personalized technical assistance.

faqs.question: What information should I provide when requesting technical support?
faqs.answer: To receive efficient technical support, please provide: 1) Complete part number and quantity, 2) Detailed description of the issue or question, 3) Application information (voltage, current, switching frequency, topology), 4) Schematic and PCB layout if applicable, 5) Operating conditions (ambient temperature, cooling method), 6) Failure mode description if troubleshooting, and 7) Any oscilloscope captures or thermal measurements. The more complete the information, the faster we can assist.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for personalized technical assistance.

faqs.question: Does LiTong provide sample programming and evaluation support?
faqs.answer: Yes, LiTong Electronics provides comprehensive evaluation support including: Free samples for qualified projects (typically 2-5 pieces), Evaluation boards for key products, Reference design documentation, Application notes and design guides, and On-site or remote technical consultation. Sample requests can be submitted through our website or through your sales contact. Typical sample lead time is 1-2 weeks from approved request.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for personalized technical assistance.

faqs.question: What is the warranty period for Xinleineng power modules?
faqs.answer: Xinleineng power modules are covered by a 24-month warranty from the date of shipment from LiTong Electronics. The warranty covers defects in materials and workmanship under normal use conditions. Warranty claims require return of the failed unit for analysis. If the failure is confirmed as a manufacturing defect, LiTong will provide replacement units at no charge. The warranty does not cover damage from misuse, overstress, improper installation, or unauthorized modifications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for personalized technical assistance.

faqs.question: How can I request a Failure Analysis (FA) for a failed power module?
faqs.answer: To request a Failure Analysis, contact LiTong technical support with the following information: 1) Part number and lot code of the failed unit, 2) Date code and serial number if available, 3) Detailed description of the failure symptoms, 4) Application circuit schematic, 5) Operating conditions at time of failure, and 6) Return the failed unit with proper ESD protection. Our FA process typically takes 2-4 weeks and includes electrical testing, X-ray inspection, decapsulation analysis, and root cause determination.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for personalized technical assistance.

faqs.question: Are there any RoHS and REACH compliance certificates available?
faqs.answer: Yes, all Xinleineng power modules are fully RoHS compliant (lead-free) and meet REACH regulations. Compliance certificates are available for download from our website or can be requested from your sales contact. The modules use Sn-Ag-Cu solder for die attach and terminal plating, with no restricted substances above threshold limits. Full material composition declarations (FMD) are available for customers requiring detailed substance information.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for personalized technical assistance.

faqs.question: What are the recommended storage conditions for unused power modules?
faqs.answer: Unused Xinleineng power modules should be stored in the original packaging in a controlled environment: Temperature between -10C and +35C, Relative humidity below 60% (non-condensing), Away from direct sunlight and corrosive atmospheres, and Protected from ESD. When stored under these conditions, the modules have a shelf life of 36 months from the date of manufacture. After opening the original packaging, modules should be used within 6 months or stored in a dry nitrogen cabinet.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for personalized technical assistance.

faqs.question: Does LiTong offer custom power module design services?
faqs.answer: Yes, for high-volume applications (typically 10,000+ units annually), LiTong can facilitate custom power module design with Xinleineng. Customization options include: Custom voltage and current ratings, Specific package outlines and pin configurations, Integrated temperature sensing, Custom lead forming and trimming, and Special testing or screening requirements. Custom development typically requires 6-12 months from specification to production. Contact our sales team to discuss your custom requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for personalized technical assistance.

faqs.question: Where can I find application notes and design guides for Xinleineng products?
faqs.answer: Application notes and design guides are available in the Documentation section of our website. Current documents include: AN-001 - Gate Drive Design for IGBT Modules, AN-002 - Thermal Management Guidelines, AN-003 - Parallel Operation of Power Modules, AN-004 - Snubber Circuit Design, AN-005 - EMC Design for Motor Drives, and AN-006 - Solar Inverter Design Guide. These documents provide detailed design guidance, calculation examples, and best practices for implementing Xinleineng power modules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for personalized technical assistance.

faqs.question: How do I handle ESD protection when working with power modules?
faqs.answer: Proper ESD protection is essential when handling power modules: 1) Work at an ESD-safe workstation with grounded mat and wrist strap, 2) Keep modules in ESD-safe packaging until ready for use, 3) Avoid touching module terminals or exposed metal surfaces, 4) Use ESD-safe tools and soldering equipment, 5) Ground yourself before handling modules, and 6) Store unused modules in anti-static bags. While power modules are more robust than small-signal devices, gate oxide damage can occur from ESD and cause latent reliability issues.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for personalized technical assistance.

articles.title: Gate Drive Design Guide for IGBT and SiC Power Modules
articles.description: Comprehensive guide to designing optimal gate drive circuits for Xinleineng IGBT and SiC power modules.
articles.content: Proper gate drive design is critical for achieving optimal performance and reliability from power modules. This guide covers gate drive voltage requirements, gate resistor selection, isolation techniques, and protection features for both IGBT and SiC MOSFET modules. Understanding these principles will help designers create robust power conversion systems with maximum efficiency and reliability.

faqs.question: What are the recommended gate voltage levels for Xinleineng IGBT modules?
faqs.answer: Xinleineng IGBT modules require +15V for turn-on and -8V for turn-off. The positive voltage ensures full channel enhancement for minimum on-state voltage drop, while the negative voltage prevents false turn-on from dv/dt induced current through the Miller capacitance. Gate voltage should be regulated to +/-1V tolerance to ensure consistent switching performance across temperature and load variations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What gate voltage is required for SiC MOSFET modules?
faqs.answer: Xinleineng SiC MOSFET modules should be driven with +18V to +20V for turn-on and -2V to -4V for turn-off. The higher positive voltage is required to achieve the low on-resistance specified in the datasheet. Unlike IGBTs, SiC MOSFETs do not have a Miller plateau, but still benefit from negative turn-off voltage to prevent parasitic turn-on. Some designs use +15V/-4V for compatibility with existing IGBT drivers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the appropriate gate resistor value?
faqs.answer: Gate resistor selection involves balancing switching speed against EMI and voltage overshoot. Start with Rg = (Vdrive - Vplateau) / Ig_peak, where Ig_peak is the driver peak current capability. For IGBT modules, typical values range from 5 to 22 ohms. Lower values provide faster switching but increase EMI and voltage overshoot. Higher values reduce EMI but increase switching losses. A good starting point is 10 ohms, then adjust based on oscilloscope measurements of switching waveforms.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of the Miller clamp in gate drive circuits?
faqs.answer: The Miller clamp provides a low-impedance path to ground during turn-off to prevent dv/dt induced turn-on. When the collector/drain voltage rises rapidly during turn-off, current flows through the Miller capacitance (Cgc) into the gate. Without a Miller clamp, this current can raise the gate voltage above the threshold voltage, causing unwanted turn-on. Active Miller clamp circuits or negative turn-off voltage are both effective solutions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What isolation voltage is required for gate drivers in power modules?
faqs.answer: Gate drivers for 1200V power modules should have minimum 2500V isolation voltage (basic insulation) or 5000V (reinforced insulation). The isolation must be rated for the working voltage between the control circuit (typically grounded) and the power circuit (switching between 0V and DC bus voltage). Reinforced isolation is recommended for safety-critical applications and provides better protection against single-point failures.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: How do I implement desaturation protection for short-circuit detection?
faqs.answer: Desaturation protection monitors the collector-emitter (or drain-source) voltage during the on-state. Under normal operation, Vce(sat) is 2-3V for IGBTs. During a short circuit, Vce rises rapidly to near the DC bus voltage. The protection circuit blanks the detection for a few microseconds after turn-on (to avoid false triggering), then monitors Vce. If Vce exceeds the threshold (typically 7-9V), the driver performs a soft shutdown to limit di/dt and voltage spikes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What PCB layout considerations are important for gate drive circuits?
faqs.answer: Key PCB layout considerations include: 1) Minimize gate loop inductance by placing the driver close to the module, 2) Use wide, short traces for gate connections, 3) Implement a Kelvin connection to the emitter/source sense terminal, 4) Place decoupling capacitors close to the driver IC, 5) Keep high dv/dt traces away from sensitive gate drive traces, and 6) Use ground planes to reduce noise coupling. Poor layout can cause oscillations, increased switching losses, and EMI issues.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: Can I use the same gate driver for both IGBT and SiC modules?
faqs.answer: Some gate drivers can be used for both IGBT and SiC modules with appropriate configuration changes. Key considerations: 1) Output voltage range must support both +15V/-8V (IGBT) and +18V/-4V (SiC), 2) Output current capability should be 6A or higher for SiC fast switching, 3) Propagation delay should be under 100ns for SiC high-frequency operation, and 4) Some drivers have programmable features to optimize for each device type. Always verify the driver datasheet specifications against module requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

articles.summary: Comprehensive guide to designing optimal gate drive circuits for Xinleineng IGBT and SiC power modules.

articles.title: Thermal Management and Heatsink Design
articles.description: Guidelines for thermal design, heatsink selection, and thermal interface materials for Xinleineng power modules.
articles.content: Effective thermal management is essential for reliable operation of power modules. This guide covers thermal resistance calculations, heatsink selection, thermal interface materials, and measurement techniques. Proper thermal design ensures junction temperatures remain within specification under all operating conditions, maximizing reliability and lifetime.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the maximum allowable thermal resistance for my application?
faqs.answer: Calculate required heatsink thermal resistance using: Rth_heatsink = (Tj_max - Ta) / P_loss - Rth_jc - Rth_ch. Where Tj_max is maximum junction temperature (typically 150C or 175C), Ta is maximum ambient temperature, P_loss is total power dissipation (conduction + switching losses), Rth_jc is junction-to-case thermal resistance from the datasheet, and Rth_ch is case-to-heatsink thermal resistance (typically 0.1-0.3C/W with thermal grease). The result gives the maximum allowable heatsink thermal resistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What thermal interface material should I use between the module and heatsink?
faqs.answer: Recommended thermal interface materials include: 1) High-performance thermal grease (0.5-1.0C-cm²/W) for best thermal performance, 2) Phase change materials for automated assembly, 3) Thermal pads for ease of handling (1.5-3.0C-cm²/W), and 4) Graphite sheets for high thermal conductivity. For best results, apply a thin, uniform layer of thermal grease (approximately 0.1mm thick) using the dot or line method, then tighten mounting screws to specified torque.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What is the recommended mounting torque for Xinleineng power modules?
faqs.answer: Xinleineng power modules should be mounted to the heatsink with a torque of 2.0 to 4.0 Nm (18-35 lb-in). Use a torque wrench to ensure consistent mounting force across all mounting holes. Insufficient torque results in high thermal contact resistance and potential hot spots. Excessive torque can damage the module package or cause warping that reduces thermal contact. Always follow the mounting sequence specified in the datasheet, typically tightening screws in a cross pattern.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: How do I measure junction temperature in a power module?
faqs.answer: Junction temperature can be measured indirectly using the internal NTC thermistor or calculated from Vce(sat) measurement. The NTC thermistor provides a direct temperature reading near the chip, but requires calibration. For Vce(sat) method: 1) Measure Vce(sat) at low current (e.g., 100mA) during operation, 2) Compare to the temperature coefficient from the datasheet (typically 2mV/C for IGBTs), 3) Calculate Tj = Tref + (Vce_measured - Vce_ref) / TC. This method provides accurate junction temperature without disturbing the circuit.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What is the difference between natural convection and forced air cooling?
faqs.answer: Natural convection relies on buoyancy-driven airflow and requires larger heatsink surface area but has no moving parts and silent operation. Typical thermal resistance is 0.5-2.0C/W for natural convection heatsinks. Forced air cooling uses fans to increase airflow, enabling smaller heatsinks with thermal resistance of 0.1-0.5C/W. Forced air requires fan power, creates noise, and has reliability considerations for the fan. Choose natural convection for reliability-critical applications below 2kW, forced air for higher power density requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: How does altitude affect thermal performance?
faqs.answer: Air density decreases with altitude, reducing the effectiveness of air-cooled heatsinks. Above 1000m elevation, natural convection capacity decreases by approximately 10% per 1000m. Forced air cooling is less affected but still requires derating above 2000m. For high-altitude applications, increase heatsink size by 20-30%, increase fan speed, or consider liquid cooling. Always verify thermal performance with actual testing at the operating altitude if possible.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What is thermal cycling and how does it affect module reliability?
faqs.answer: Thermal cycling occurs when the module temperature changes due to load variations or on/off operation. Repeated thermal cycling causes mechanical stress from coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) mismatches between materials, potentially leading to solder joint fatigue and wire bond degradation. Minimize thermal cycling by: 1) Maintaining stable operating temperature, 2) Using soft-start to limit inrush heating, 3) Avoiding frequent on/off cycling, and 4) Ensuring adequate thermal mass in the heatsink. Xinleineng modules are qualified to IEC 60747-9 for thermal cycling endurance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: When should I consider liquid cooling instead of air cooling?
faqs.answer: Consider liquid cooling when: 1) Power dissipation exceeds 500W per module, 2) Ambient temperature is high (>50C), 3) Airflow is restricted or contaminated, 4) Silent operation is required, or 5) Very high power density is needed. Liquid cooling can achieve thermal resistance of 0.01-0.05C/W, enabling much higher power handling than air cooling. However, liquid cooling adds system complexity with pumps, plumbing, and coolant maintenance. For most applications below 50kW, forced air cooling is sufficient and more cost-effective.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

articles.summary: Guidelines for thermal design, heatsink selection, and thermal interface materials for Xinleineng power modules.

articles.title: Parallel Operation of Power Modules
articles.description: Guidelines for paralleling IGBT and SiC power modules to achieve higher current capability.
articles.content: Paralleling power modules allows designers to achieve current ratings beyond individual module capabilities. This guide covers static and dynamic current sharing, gate drive considerations, layout requirements, and protection strategies for reliable parallel operation of Xinleineng power modules.

faqs.question: What are the main challenges when paralleling power modules?
faqs.answer: The main challenges in paralleling power modules are: 1) Static current sharing - ensuring equal current distribution during steady-state conduction, 2) Dynamic current sharing - preventing current imbalance during switching transitions, 3) Thermal imbalance from uneven current distribution, and 4) Protection coordination to prevent cascade failures. These challenges arise from variations in device characteristics, circuit parasitics, and thermal conditions between parallel paths.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: How can I achieve good static current sharing?
faqs.answer: Good static current sharing requires matched device characteristics and symmetric layout: 1) Select modules from the same production lot to minimize Vce(sat) variation, 2) Use small series resistors (0.01-0.1 ohm) in each emitter/source path to provide negative feedback, 3) Ensure equal thermal conditions for all modules on a common heatsink, and 4) Use a common gate driver with individual gate resistors for each module. With these measures, current imbalance can be kept within 10%.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What layout techniques improve dynamic current sharing?
faqs.answer: For good dynamic current sharing: 1) Use symmetrical PCB layout with equal trace lengths and impedances to all modules, 2) Implement Kelvin source connections for each module to minimize common source inductance, 3) Place modules close together to reduce loop inductance differences, 4) Use laminated busbars for DC link connections to minimize parasitic inductance, and 5) Consider using small ferrite beads on individual gate leads to dampen oscillations. Simulation of the layout parasitics is recommended before building prototypes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: Should I use separate gate drivers or a common driver for paralleled modules?
faqs.answer: For two modules in parallel, a common gate driver with individual gate resistors is usually sufficient. For three or more modules, separate but synchronized gate drivers provide better control. Separate drivers allow individual adjustment of gate timing to compensate for layout asymmetries. If using separate drivers, ensure they have matched propagation delays (within 20ns) and use a common PWM signal to maintain synchronization. Always include individual desaturation protection for each module.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: How do I protect paralleled modules against short circuits?
faqs.answer: Short circuit protection for paralleled modules requires: 1) Individual desaturation detection for each module to identify which module sees the fault, 2) Soft shutdown of all modules simultaneously to prevent voltage overshoots, 3) Current sensors in each parallel path for overcurrent detection, and 4) Fuse or circuit breaker coordination to isolate faulted modules. Do not rely on a single protection circuit for multiple paralleled modules, as current imbalance may prevent timely detection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What is the maximum number of modules that can be paralleled?
faqs.answer: While there is no absolute limit, practical considerations typically limit paralleling to 2-4 modules. With more modules, achieving good current sharing becomes increasingly difficult due to layout constraints and device variations. For very high current requirements, consider using higher current rated modules or custom modules instead of paralleling many standard modules. If paralleling more than 4 modules is necessary, extensive characterization and derating (typically 10-15%) is recommended.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: Can I parallel IGBT and SiC modules together?
faqs.answer: Paralleling IGBT and SiC modules is not recommended due to their different switching characteristics. SiC MOSFETs switch much faster than IGBTs, which would result in severe dynamic current imbalance and potential device failure during switching transitions. If mixed technologies are required in a system, use them in separate converter stages (e.g., SiC for high-frequency DC-DC, IGBT for line-frequency inverter) rather than paralleling them in the same switch position.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

articles.summary: Guidelines for paralleling IGBT and SiC power modules to achieve higher current capability.

articles.title: Troubleshooting Common Power Module Issues
articles.description: Diagnostic procedures and solutions for common problems encountered with power modules.
articles.content: This troubleshooting guide addresses the most common issues encountered when using power modules, including unexpected failures, overheating, EMI problems, and gate drive issues. Systematic diagnostic procedures help identify root causes and implement effective solutions.

faqs.question: What causes unexpected power module failures in new designs?
faqs.answer: Common causes of unexpected failures include: 1) Insufficient voltage derating - spikes exceeding voltage ratings, 2) Inadequate thermal design - junction temperatures exceeding maximum ratings, 3) Gate drive issues - insufficient voltage, slow switching, or oscillations, 4) Shoot-through from insufficient dead time in bridge configurations, 5) Overcurrent from load faults without proper protection, and 6) ESD damage during assembly. Use the checklist in AN-007 for systematic failure analysis.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: How do I diagnose overheating issues?
faqs.answer: To diagnose overheating: 1) Measure actual junction temperature using Vce(sat) method or thermistor, 2) Verify heatsink thermal resistance matches calculations, 3) Check thermal interface material application and mounting torque, 4) Measure airflow for forced-air cooled systems, 5) Calculate actual power losses and compare to thermal design assumptions, 6) Check for blocked airflow or dust accumulation, and 7) Verify switching frequency and duty cycle match design values. Common fixes include larger heatsinks, better TIM, or increased airflow.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What causes gate voltage oscillations and how do I fix them?
faqs.answer: Gate voltage oscillations are typically caused by: 1) High gate loop inductance from long traces or wires, 2) Insufficient gate resistance causing underdamped switching, 3) Common source inductance coupling switching transients into the gate circuit, 4) Insufficient decoupling capacitance on the gate driver supply, and 5) Poor grounding or ground bounce. Solutions include: shorter gate traces, increased gate resistance (try 22-47 ohms), Kelvin source connection, additional decoupling capacitors (100nF ceramic), and improved PCB grounding.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: How do I identify and fix EMI issues caused by power modules?
faqs.answer: EMI issues manifest as conducted or radiated emissions exceeding limits. Diagnostic steps: 1) Identify frequency spectrum of emissions using spectrum analyzer, 2) Check for proper grounding and shielding, 3) Verify dv/dt control through gate resistor selection, 4) Inspect filter component placement and values, 5) Check for common-mode current paths. Common solutions: increase gate resistance to slow switching edges, add RC snubbers across module terminals, improve input/output filtering, use shielded cables, and implement proper grounding practices.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What causes high switching losses and how can I reduce them?
faqs.answer: High switching losses result from: 1) Slow switching due to weak gate drivers or high gate resistance, 2) High stray inductance causing voltage overshoot and extended switching times, 3) Insufficient negative gate voltage for IGBTs, 4) Operating at higher switching frequency than designed, and 5) High DC bus voltage increasing switching energy. Reduce losses by: using stronger gate drivers, optimizing gate resistance (balance against EMI), minimizing stray inductance in layout, ensuring proper gate voltage levels, and verifying switching frequency settings.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: How do I troubleshoot desaturation protection false trips?
faqs.answer: False desaturation trips can be caused by: 1) Blanking time too short for normal switching, 2) Desaturation threshold set too low, 3) Gate voltage too low causing high Vce(sat), 4) Excessive lead inductance causing voltage overshoot, and 5) Module operating outside safe operating area. Solutions: increase blanking time (typically 2-5 microseconds), verify desat threshold against datasheet Vce(sat), check gate voltage under load, reduce stray inductance, and verify operating conditions are within SOA curves.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: What should I check if the module shows high leakage current?
faqs.answer: High leakage current may indicate: 1) ESD damage to gate oxide, 2) Overvoltage stress causing degradation, 3) Thermal runaway from poor cooling, 4) Contamination on module surface, or 5) Normal behavior at high temperature (exponential increase with temperature). Check: gate threshold voltage for ESD damage, voltage spikes with oscilloscope, junction temperature, module cleanliness, and compare leakage to datasheet values at measured temperature. If leakage exceeds datasheet limits at room temperature, the module may be damaged and should be replaced.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

faqs.question: How do I verify if a module is damaged after a failure event?
faqs.answer: To check for module damage: 1) Measure gate-emitter resistance (should be >1M ohm for IGBT, >100k for SiC), 2) Check for gate oxide breakdown (low resistance indicates damage), 3) Measure collector-emitter leakage at rated voltage (should be <1mA at 25C), 4) Check Vce(sat) at low current and compare to datasheet, 5) Inspect for physical damage (cracks, burning, discoloration), and 6) X-ray inspection for internal wire bond or die attach damage. Document all measurements for failure analysis submission if warranty claim is needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for detailed technical guidance.

articles.summary: Diagnostic procedures and solutions for common problems encountered with power modules.
